coredata.dc:title: Photo-generation of mercury cold vapor mediated by graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite: On line time-resolved speciation at ultra-trace levels
coredata.dc:creator: Miranda-Andrades, Jarol R.
Letichevsky, Sonia
González Larrudé, Dunieskys R.
Aucelio, Ricardo Q.
coredata.dc:description: Mercury speciation was achieved using a nanocomposite, consisting of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles, to mediate photo-degradation of mercurial species into the Hg cold vapor detected by atomic spectrometry. Sample solution (containing Hg2+, CH3CH2Hg, and CH3Hg at hundreds of ng L−1) was placed in quartz tube containing formic acid solution (2% v/v) and microliter aliquot of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite dispersion (0.6 mg of nanocomposite). The tube was placed inside a photochemical reactor then, adapted to the mercury-dedicated spectrometer. Quantitative speciation was achieved taking advantage of the differences in UV photodegradation kinetics: Hg2+ (5 min), CH3CH2Hg (9 min) and CH3Hg (13 min). Gas-chromatography cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry was used to confirm the evolution of the reactions over time during photo-reaction. The limits of detection were 10 ng L−1 for CH3CH2Hg and 7 ng L−1 for Hg2+ and CH3Hg.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury speciation was achieved using a nanocomposite, consisting of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles, to mediate photo-degradation of mercurial species into the Hg cold vapor detected by atomic spectrometry. Sample solution (containing Hg2+, CH3CH2Hg, and CH3Hg at hundreds of ng L−1) was placed in quartz tube containing formic acid solution (2% v/v) and microliter aliquot of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite dispersion (0.6 mg of nanocomposite). The tube was placed inside a photochemical reactor then, adapted to the mercury-dedicated spectrometer. Quantitative speciation was achieved taking advantage of the differences in UV photodegradation kinetics: Hg2+ (5 min), CH3CH2Hg (9 min) and CH3Hg (13 min). Gas-chromatography cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry was used to confirm the evolution of the reactions over time during photo-reaction. The limits of detection were 10 ng L−1 for CH3CH2Hg and 7 ng L−1 for Hg2+ and CH3Hg.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is an environmental pollutant found as elemental mercury (Hg0), inorganic mercury (mainly as Hg2+), and organic mercury, more significantly as methylmercury (CH3Hg) [1]. Organic mercury is more dangerous because of their favourable oil partition that favour its accumulation in tissues [2]. Methylmercury is formed from the biomethylation of Hg2+ mediated by sulphate reducing bacteria in anoxic environments [3].

Speciation of mercury in biological and environmental samples are essential to provide simultaneous and accurate determination and reliable information for the understanding of biogeochemistry and ecotoxicology of mercury. Therefore, sensitive analytical methods, capable of analysing mercury speciation at natural levels, are in high demand. Literature describes several analytical methods developed for speciation analysis of mercury as recently summarized in reviews [4–8]. Conventional speciation methods include gas chromatography (GC) with electron capture detector [9], mass spectrometry (MS) [10], or coupled to optical emission spectrometry [11]. Speciation has also been achieved by inductively coupled plasma mass atomic spectrometry (ICP-MS) hyphenated with high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12–16] and with capillary electrophoresis [17]. The conversion of aqueous mercury species to elemental vapor (Hg0) or other volatile species [18] allows analytes to be separated from the interfering matrix components in sample solutions. As a result, sensitivity and selectivity have been improved, leading to a wide implementation in atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [19], atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) [20], microwave induced plasma atomic emission spectrometry [21] and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP˗OES) [22].

Effective mercury determination and speciation methods have been proposed chemical cold vapor (CV) generation [23], CV generation using microwave [24] or UV induced CV [25–27]. CV-AFS [28,29] has been coupled with GC [30,31] to separate derivatized mercury species, as the EPA Method 1630 (Methylmercury in water by distillation, aqueous ethylation, purge and trap, and cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry). Chemical derivatization is often made by ethylation or propylation being affected by pH, organic matter and ionic force [32–34]. CV-AFS has also been coupled with flow-injection analysis [35]. Indirect methods based on CV atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [19] are also convenient as it measures inorganic mercury and then total mercury (after chemical or photochemical oxidation) [24,36,37]. Although less sensitive and providing limited speciation, CV˗AAS are simpler and cheaper and may be adequate to attend most legislations on mercury control.

Exposure to UV, in the presence of free radical-generating reagents, accelerates the reduction of Hg2+, eliminating the need for SnCl2 or NaBH4 as chemical reducing agents [26,38]. TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2-NPs) have often been used to achieve more efficiency in the UV-induced catalytic production of Hg0 from mercury species. Formic acid has been used as hole scavenger to reduce efficiency of nanomaterial excitonic recombination. In this way, mercury species have been detected by AFS after separation in a HPLC column, enabling limits of detection (LOD) of 10 ng L−1 for Hg2+ and 20 ng L− 1 for CH3Hg [39]. Inorganic mercury has also been determined by AFS with LOD of 40 ng L−1 [40] and CV-AAS with LOD of 50 ng L−1 [41] after mercury CV photo˗generation using TiO2-NPs with the aid of low molecular weight alcohols, aldehydes and carboxylic acids to promote the cleavage of C-Hg bonding [40,41]. A similar work has been made using ZrO2 NPs [42]. Ion chromatography was used to separate Hg2+ and CH3Hg prior to the UV photochemical treatment using TiO2-NPs prior to ICP-MS determination, providing LOD of 30 ng L−1 (CH3Hg) and 100 ng L−1 (Hg2+) [43].

Hyphenation of a photo-induced chemical CV generator, HPLC and AFS was employed for the speciation analysis of mercury [44]. The photodecomposition of organomercurial compounds and reduction of Hg2+ to mercury vapor was performed by microwave/UV irradiation aided by the catalytic action of TiO2 NPs and achieved LOD were 150, 150 and 350 ng L−1 for Hg2+, CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg, respectively [44]. Similarly, a method for speciation of mercury using AFS after pre-concentration and photochemical CV generation has been reported [45]. Speciation of mercury was obtained by using diethyldithiocarbamate as a chelating agent for pre-concentration and to aid photo-conversion of mercury species (Hg2+ and CH3Hg) in mercury vapor prior to AFS detection and LOD achieved were 4 ng L−1 [45].

Mercury speciation using HPLC-ICP-MS, CV-AAS and GC-MS and gas chromatography-triple quadrupole mass spectrometry (GC-MS/MS) has also been achieved by using other nanomaterials or special materials to previously adsorb total mercurial species or to selectively adsorb either inorganic or organic mercury species [46–50]. For instance, multi-wall carbon nanotubes [51], fullerene (C60) [49,52], 1,5-bis(2-pyridyl) methylene thiocarbohydrazide) functionalized with mesoporous SiO2 nanoparticles [53], γ-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane capped Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles [54], SiO2 beads decorated with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [55], magnetic nanoparticles functionalized silver nanoparticles (AgNPs@Fe3O4) functionalized with either 2-mercaptoethane sulphonate (for inorganic mercury) or with L-cysteine (for the organic mercury species) [56].

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor materials of nanometric size that show narrow bandgap between valence discrete band levels and conductance discrete band levels produced due to quantum confinement. They mediate redox processes and photo-degradation due to the production of free radicals and, when dispersed in aqueous solution, QDs present large surface area to interact with other substances. For instance, photo-degrading of dyes and phenols has been achieved using CdS QDs (coated with polyvinyl chloride) [57]. However, inorganic QDs dots are usually formed by toxic elements and are prone to oxidation and aggregation when dispersed in aqueous solution.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometric fragments of graphene with semiconductor properties, presenting a wide extinction spectral range that covers near UV and part of the visible spectrum. Recently, the catalytic effect of GQDs on the photo-degradation of fuchsin dye, by visible light, was demonstrated [58]. The visible light photo-degradation of thiomersal was achieved using GQDs, producing Hg2+ that was further reduced to be determined by CV-AAS [59]. Other works reported the interaction between the negatively charged GQD surface and Hg2+ affecting the excitonic nanomaterial recombination [60,61].

TiO2 NPs have been associated to many nanomaterials, such as the ones made of noble metals, to improve photocatalytic activity [39,43] but despite the fact that GQDs present photocatalytic activity [58,59] there are few reports associating GQDs to TiO2 NPs aiming to improve its photocatalytic activity, for instance to degrade methylene blue under UV [62], to promote efficient hydrogen evolution through photocatalysis [63], or to promote photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B under visible light [64].

In this work, ultra-trace quantification of mercury species (Hg2+, CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg) was achieved after efficient photo-degradation assisted by a nanocomposite (NC) consisting of GQDs and TiO2 NPs. Due to the different kinetics in photocatalytic reactions, speciation could be achieved without chromatographic separation and with CV˗AAS detection using a portable mercury dedicated system.

Ultrapure water (resistivity <18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q A10 Gradient system (Millipore, USA). Titanium (IV) oxide nanopowder (P25-TiO2, mixture of rutile and anatase, with 21 nm average particle size) was from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Sodium tetra(n-propyl) borate, NaB(CH2CH2CH3)4 at 99% purity, the high purity standards (at 1 mg L−1) of methylmercury chloride (CH3HgCl), ethylmercury chloride (CH3CH2HgCl) and mercuric chloride (HgCl2) and the high purity buffer solution (pH 4.5) were supplied by Brooks Rand Instruments (USA). Tin chloride was supplied by Vetec (Brazil). Citric acid, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, ammonium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide (30% in volume) and formic acid were purchased from Merck (Germany). The standard solution NIST-SRM 1641c (mercury in water) was used for accuracy tests. Nitrogen (99.99%) and argon (99.999%) were obtained from Lynde (Brazil).

Photocatalytic reactions were carried out in 15 mL hermetically closed quartz tubes directly adapted, by means of a 5 mm diameter transparent flexible silicon hose, to the model RA-915 mercury dedicated atomic absorption spectrometer (Lumex, Russia). The spectrometer has an optical multi-path length cell and Zeeman background correction. This system was connected to a RP-92 chemical reduction accessory for aqueous solutions where air flow was washed (in NaOH 10% m/v) before entering the spectrometer. The laboratory-built photo-reactor consisted of six commercial mercury fluorescent lamps (main line at 253 nm and secondary lines in the 296–313 nm range) placed on the inner wall of a PVC tube (20 cm diameter and 30 cm width). A small fan, placed on top of the reactor, maintained internal temperature below 30 °C. A quartz tube, containing sample, was adjusted in a rack, on the top of a small magnetic stirrer, and placed in the center of the reactor. In order to keep solutions, inside the quartz tube, in continuous agitation, a mini-magnetic bar was inserted inside tube and rotation adjusted to maintain uniform and slow agitation. Mercury speciation comparative studies were made on a GC speciation system coupled to a CV-AFS detector (Model III Brooks Rand Instruments, USA). The GC column (Brooks Rand Instruments), used to separate the propylated mercury species, was a U shaped glass column (4 mm internal diameter) packed with 15% OV-3 (phenyl methyl dimethyl silicone 10%) in Chromasorb White.

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Lambda 35 model PerkinElmer double beam spectrophotometer (500 nm min−1 scan rate and 10 nm spectral band pass). Photoluminescence measurements were made using a PerkinElmer LS 55 model luminescence spectrophotometer (1200 nm min−1 scan rate and 10 nm spectral band pass). Nanoparticle images were obtained using a field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, model JSM-7800F, Japan). Images were obtained from dried dispersions placed onto carbon-coated transmission electron microscopy (TEM) grid for images in scanning transmission mode (STEM) at 30 kV or placed in a double sided conductive adhesive carbon tape for images in conventional scanning electron microscope (SEM) images at 15 kV. The working distance of 6 mm for STEM images and 3 mm for SEM images and in both cases using a secondary electron detector. Raman spectra were obtained using a confocal microscope (Horiba Xplora, Japan) equipped with a 532 nm laser source. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies of the GQDs/TiO2 were made on a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) equipped with copper tube (λ = 1.5418 nm) operating at 40 kV and 40 mA, Ni filter and Lynx Eye detector. The Bragg-Brentano geometry was employed and the scan was performed from 2θ = 10° to 90° with 0.02° of step. A MPA 210 model pH-meter (Tecnopon, Brazil) was used to measure pH with a glass membrane electrode combined with an Ag|AgCl(KClsat) reference electrode.

The GQDs supported on TiO2 NPs were produced by the pyrolysis of (1.0 g) citric acid in the presence of (30 mg) TiO2 NPs in 5 mL beaker. The mixture was heated to about 240 °C in order to melt the citric acid. After about 2 min, the hot melted liquid mixture (pale yellow to brownish) was poured into 50 mL of NaOH solution (0.25 mol L−1) at room temperature to form an aqueous dispersion. The dispersion was kept under stirring for 24 h. Then, this original GQDs/TiO2 NC dispersion was centrifuged (at 11.200 RFC) for 20 min. The solid pellet was separated from the supernatant and dried, under vacuum, at 60 °C for 10 h.

Standard solutions of CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg and Hg2+ (at 1000 ng L−1) were prepared by sequential dilution of standard solutions in amber volumetric flasks (stored in the refrigerator protected from light). The alkaline solution of the derivatizing agent was made by dissolving 0.05 g of the NaB(CH2CH2CH2CH3)4 commercial solution (at 1% m/v) in 2.5 mL of KOH (2% v/v), adjusting the final volume to 5.00 mL with water and storing at 5 °C. The SnCl2 solution (20% w/v) was prepared concentrated HCl. The solution was heated to reduce the volume to about 50 mL in order to eliminate mercury contamination. After cooling, the final volume was adjusted with ultrapure water. Aqueous dispersions of GQDs/TiO2 NC were prepared by dispersing 30 mg of the solid in 10 mL of water under a brief vigorous vortex mixing followed by 10 min sonication. Prior to use, the dispersion was placed in a glass washing bottle to be purged with nitrogen (15 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mercury speciation using photo-reactions mediated by GQDs/TiO2 NC

ce:section.ce:para: Volumes of 100 μL of samples or standard solutions were transferred to a quartz tube (connected to the CV-AAS system) containing 5.00 mL of solution containing H2O2 (1% v/v), formic acid (1% m/v) with pH adjusted to 3.0 (by adding HCl 0.1 mol L−1). Then, 100 μL of the GQDs/TiO2 NC dispersion (3 mg mL−1 of C) was added. A magnetic bar was inserted and the tube placed inside the photo-reactor, positioning it in the center of the device and on the top of the magnetic stirrer. After 1 min (Hg2+), 3 min (CH3Hg) and 6 min (CH3CH2Hg) of UV exposure, the generated Hg0 was transferred, through a controlled air flow (3 mL min−1), to the CV-AAS system operating in continuous acquisition mode with conditions adjusted according Table 1 . Prior to each analysis, the system was flushed with air, passing through a tube containing ultrapure water, until residual Hg signal reached baseline level. A scheme of the experimental set up used in this work is presented in Fig. S1A along with the traditional set up used in the method based on chemical reduction using SnCl2 (Fig. S1B).

Speciation kinetic studies were made using the GC-CV-AFS. Aliquots (0.10 mL) of standard solutions of the mercurial species were mixed with GQDs/TiO2 NC and exposed to UV in closed quartz tubes. Then they were transferred to 40 mL amber vials, with septum caps, containing 20 mL of ultrapure water, 0.2 mL of the acetate buffer solution and 0.10 mL of a NaB(CH2CH2CH3)4 aqueous solution (1% v/v). After 15 min, CH3Hg, CH3H2Hg and Hg2+ were respectively converted into the volatile methyl(n-propyl)mercury, CH3HgCH2CH2CH3, ethyl(n-propyl)mercury, CH3H2HgCH2CH2CH3, and di-n-propyl mercury, Hg(CH2CH2CH3)2. These flasks were placed into the automatic sampler to be transferred to a glass cell where it was bubbled with an argon flow (312 mL min−1) that carried the volatile species to the chromatographic module. The GC column temperature was 36 °C and Ar mobile phase flow was 30 mL min−1. As separation occurred, the separated zones of the propylated mercury species were thermally decomposed into Hg0 before reaching the dedicated fluorescence detection system.

The GQDs dispersion (to be mixed with the TiO2 NPs, forming the GQDs/TiO2 NC) was produced by hydro-exfoliation in ultrapure water. The diluted GQDs dispersion (50 times in water) showed extinction that covered the violet-blue range with a maximum peak at about 200 nm and first excitonic shoulder at 345 nm (Fig. 1 A spectrum a). Extinction from GQDs/TiO2 NC diluted dispersions presented a hypsochrmic shift compared to the one of GQDs, in this case with first excitonic at 335 nm (Fig. 1A spectrum b). The diluted GQDs dispersion (100 times in water) produced intense photoluminescence (maximum at 473 nm) under excitation at 345 nm (Fig. 1B, spectrum a). The formation of a differentiated nanomaterial, when mixing GQDs and TiO2, is confirmed by the hypsochromic shift of the emission maximum (456 nm) (Fig. 1B, spectrum b). Quantum yield of these GQDs was about 21% at 25 °C. Even after six months of storage (under refrigeration), the GQDs/TiO2 NC dispersion is stable and produces strong photoluminescence (no spectral shifting).

FTIR spectra of GQDs/TiO2 NC presented wide absorption bands at 3000-3500 cm−1 attributed to O-H elongation (Fig. 1C), suggesting presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of nanomaterial. The strong peak at 1671 cm−1 is associated with the C=O stretch vibration. The Ti-O and Ti-O-Ti band stretching are observed in the range of 500–700 cm−1. Results also indicated that GQDs/TiO2 NC present surface -COOH groups that stabilize nanoparticles in aqueous system and may lead to improved photocatalysis [65,66]. Raman spectra for GQDs and GQDs/TiO2 NC present main characteristic peak of TiO2 NPs (at 155 cm−1) attributed to the main Eg(1) anatase vibration mode. The presence of crystalline TiO2 is confirmed by vibration peaks at 100 cm−1 for B1g(1), at 516 cm−1 for A1g(1) and B1g(2) and at 639 cm−1 for Eg(2) [67,68]. The two bands, at 1343 cm−1 (D band attributed to edges, defects and disordered carbon) and at 1570 cm−1 (G band arising from the E2g mode relative to the ordered sp2 bond carbon atoms) correspond to the presence of GQDs (Fig. 1D). The presence of D and G bands along with TiO2 NP bands indicate a possible strong interaction between GQDs and TiO2 as previously shown by FTIR [69].

From SEM image (Fig. 2 ), the TiO2 nanoparticles average size (estimated from 100 nanoparticles from a solid sample) was 20.3 nm but distribution includes small fractions of nanoparticle as small as a few nanometers and fractions up to 50 nm range. In the case of the GQDs/TiO2 NC, STEM images (Fig. 2) were obtained from a dried aqueous dispersion with distribution of sizes similar to the distribution observed for the TiO2 NPs. Average size was 19.5 nm (estimated from a small sampling of sizes) shows nanoparticles up to 51.7 nm, along with small ones in the range below 10 nm.

A profile refinement was performed to the X-ray diffraction patterns of samples (TiO2 NPs and GQDs/TiO2 NC) as seen in Fig. S2. Parameters are depicted in Table S1. The Rietveld analysis revealed that both samples are composed of 88% (w/w) anatase (JCPDS # 21-1272) and 12% (w/w) rutile (JCPDS # 21 -1276). It could be noticed that the GQDs deposition causes a reduction in the lattice parameters a and c of both anatase and rutile phases. Such a lattice contraction is related to the defects induced by the GQDs or the substitution due to its reduced size compared to Ti. Similar results were reported by Kim et al. [70]. No peak related to the carbonaceous component was detected in the structure of GQDs/TiO2 NC probably due to the low concentration (below the XRD limit detection) or due to their incorporation into the TiO2 lattice. It can also be observed that the mean crystallite sizes are similar to the crystal sizes detected by STEM. Futhermore, there was no significant change in the mean crystallite size of anatase or rutile after the carbon deposition, also corroborating the STEM results.

Photocatalytic activities of the GQDs/TiO2 NC and TiO2 NPs in reducing Hg2+ were compared as the rate of degradation of a standard aqueous solution of Hg2+ (1 μg L− 1) under exposure to UV in acidic condition (about pH 4). Aliquots of the standard Hg2+ solution (containing 5 ng of mercury) were transferred to quartz tubes, on the top of a magnetic stirrer plate, inside a photo-reactor. Tube was sealed with a rubber stopper (foiled with a Teflon tape) containing two openings, one for a glass tube connected to the CV-AAS system (by means of a silicone tube) and the other for an L-shaped glass tube, connected to a glass stopcock, that was used to open or close the system to the air. The end of the L-shaped glass tube inside the quartz tube was kept about 2 cm above the solution in order to prevent bubbling. The solution was stirred and exposed to the UV, during specific times, before turning on the pump of the CV-AAS system, then opening the system to the air, promoting continuous forced airflow to carry the mercury CV to the spectrometer. Aliquots of 200 μL of a nanomaterial dispersion (GQDs/TiO2 NC or TiO2 NPs, prepared by adding 30 mg of nanomaterial in 5 mL water) were added to the solution under continuous stirring. The efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction was evaluated by measuring the Hg0 CV generated from the photocatalytic degradation of Hg2+ within the time interval of 70 min. As a quantitative reference, the mercury CV produced by chemical reduction (using SnCl2 with no addition of nanomaterial and no UV exposure) was also measured.

Exposure of the solutions to radiation in the visible range, in the presence of the nanomaterials, does not promote significant reduction of Hg2+. Under UV, the Hg measured values (in terms of relative signal) observed after exposure to the UV are shown in Fig. 3 A along with the reference value obtained by the chemical reduction using SnCl2. A more significant photocatalytic efficiency was found for GQDs/TiO2 NC when compared to TiO2 NPs. The estimated rate of Hg2+ reduction obtained using GQDs/TiO2 NC (0.072 ng min−1, calculated from profile b of Fig. 3A) was about 2.9 times the one observed using TiO2 NPs (0.025 ng min−1, calculated from profile c of Fig. 3A). It was also found that maximum reduction using GQDs/TiO2 NC was obtained after 50 min of UV exposure while TiO2 NP took 60 min to reach maximum reduction. It is also important to point out that when GQDs dispersions when exposed to the UV, the nanomaterials tend to aggregate, forming bulk carbon particles with no quantum confinement properties [59] and, therefore, hindering its ability to catalyse the reduction of Hg2+ (Fig. 3A profile d).

Taking into consideration the total mercury content in solution and the reference signal obtained using chemical reduction (point a in Fig. 3A), the maximum efficiency in reduction was 67% in the presence of GQDs/TiO2 NC and 31% in the presence of TiO2 NPs. Such a result indicates that the modification of TiO2 NPs with GQDs improved catalytic properties. Furthermore, the association of GQDs and TiO2 NPs (probably decorating the surface of TiO2 NPs) minimized the aggregation of carbon nanoparticles under UV. Temporal profiles for Hg0 achieved by using nanomaterials are compared to the one obtained using chemical reduction by SnCl2 in Fig. 3B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumental conditions to measure mercury cold vapor produced by photocatalysis using GQDs/TiO2 NC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to promote photocatalytic reaction of Hg2+, nanomaterials were kept evenly dispersed by magnetic stirring rather than by the bubbling of the solution with forced air since stirring was found better to control reproducibility of the process. Aiming to maximize efficiency of the CV-AAS spectrometer (attached to the reaction cell) in collecting the formed Hg0 CV, chemical reduction of Hg2+ (1 μg L−1 standard solution) by SnCl2 20% v/v (no nanomaterial added) were performed using the quartz tube cell (Fig. S1A) under stirring and compared to the traditional reduction procedure using bubbler cell (Fig. S1B). Before the addition of standard solution (5 mL), the reducing reagent (2 mL) was placed into the quartz tube and then stirred (during 1 min), with the magnetic bar, to remove any residual mercury. The same procedure was made when using the bubbler cell by bubbling the reducing reagent for 1 min after addition of the standard Hg2+ solution.

The transport of Hg0 formed in the quartz tube cell was optimized by adjusting the airflow of the CV-AAS system (system pump flow rate) aiming to achieve the typical mercury time profile obtained with the bubbler cell with air flow rate at 1 mL min−1 (Fig. S3, time profile a). A similar profile (about 94% in area) was achieved using a flow rate of 3 mL min−1 (Fig. S3, time profile b). As the release of mercury from solution under stirring was slower when compared with the bubbler, a higher suction flow rate was required, and a delayed time profile was obtained.

As GQDs/TiO2 NC was efficient in promoting photo-reduction of Hg2+ under UV, the photocatalytic effect of GQDs/TiO2 NC upon organic mercurial species (CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg) was also studied. The photocatalytic activities were evaluated as degradation rates of standard aqueous solutions of CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg (aliquots equivalent to 5 ng of organomercury) under UV, at the original pH of the solutions (about pH 6). The efficiency of reactions was assessed by the Hg0 CV generated upon UV exposure within 5–70 min before opening the quartz tube for the system for signal acquisition. Aliquots of 200 μL nanomaterial dispersions (GQDs/TiO2 NC or TiO2 NPs) were added and kept under continuous stirring, inside a photo-reactor. A reference signal was obtained from the chemical reduction of Hg2+ (using SnCl2 without addition of nanomaterials and no UV exposure).

The exposure of organic mercurial species, in the presence of either TiO2 NPs or GQDs/TiO2 NC produced Hg0 similar to the background levels obtained from standard solutions exposed to visible radiation in the absence of nanomaterial. However, under UV radiation, nanomaterials promoted the formation of Hg0 from both the mercurial species as seen from the recovered mercury (in relative signal) in function of UV exposure time and temporal profiles of the generated mercury vapor during signal acquisition (Fig. 4 ).

The GQDs/TiO2 NC was more efficient in degrading the organic mercurial species than TiO2 NPs, in both cases, CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg, producing maximum intensity of measured Hg0 after 50 min of UV exposure. The CH3Hg photo-degradation rate (estimated by the sensitivity of the best linear fit between the mercury amounts recovered between 10 and 50 min of UV exposure) was 2.7 times higher in the presence of GQDs/TiO2 NC (19 ng min−1) than in the presence of TiO2 NPs (7 ng min−1).

In terms of the recovered Hg0 (taking as a reference the value obtained using chemical reduction) the use of GQDs/TiO2 NC produced 39.1% and TiO2 NPs only 15.1% of the total mercury content in solution (percent values corrected based on mercury content in CH3Hg). For CH3CH2Hg, the photo-degradation rate observed using GQDs/TiO2 NC (20 ng min−1) was 3.3 times higher in the presence of TiO2 NPs (6 ng min− 1). Recovered Hg0 from CH3CH2Hg were 49.7% (GQDs/TiO2 NC) and 16.3% (TiO2 NPs) of the Hg0 measured from the mercury content (percent values corrected based on mercury content in CH3CH2Hg).

In order to understand the UV induced photocatalytic reactions promoted by GQDs/TiO2 NC, a speciation study was performed using a gas-chromatographic system coupled with atomic fluorescence spectrometry (GC˗CV˗AFS) with a dedicated mercury CV detector. For the experiments made using either CH3Hg or CH3CH2Hg, an aliquot of mercurial standard (equivalent to 150 pg and 300 pg for CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg respectively) was introduced into the system to be derivatized, with the propylation agent, in order to obtain a chromatographic reference as seen in Fig. S4A and Fig. S4B. The reference chromatogram showed typical peaks for CH3Hg (at 1.86 ± 0.01 min) and CH3CH2Hg (4.37 ± 0.02 min) and residual reduced mercury (consisting mostly of background Hg0 and artifact Hg0) at (0.38 ± 0.02 min). After these standard solutions have been exposed to UV (25 min for CH3Hg and 15 min for CH3CH2Hg), in the presence of GQDs/TiO2 NC (before transferring to the GC-CV-AFS system to be propylated, separated and detected as Hg0), the profile showed significant decreasing of organic mercurial content, the appearance of the typical peak of Hg2+ and a significant increase of the Hg0 peak magnitude. The overall result indicated that GQDs˗TiO2 NC promotes photo-degradation of organic mercury into Hg2+ probably due to the formation of ⋅OH free radicals. Upon UV, the formed GQDs excitonic becomes a source of electrons to improve TiO2 NPs efficiency in promoting the reduction of Hg2+.

When comparing temporal profiles of the photo-generated Hg0 (under UV in the presence of GQDs/TiO2 NC), it is clear that a difference in the initial releasing of Hg0, starting at 1.67 min from Hg2+, 2.12 min from CH3CH2Hg and 2.33 min from CH3Hg, which indicated that upon further optimizations, a potential speciation through temporal separation can be achieved (Fig. S4C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic conditions in function of pH and GQDs/TiO2 NC content
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming maximization in photo-reaction of the organic mercurial species, also enabling speciation through selective time-delay in the releasing of Hg0, a circumscribed central composite design (CCD) was used to evaluate the role of the GQDs/TiO2 NC in the photocatalytic processes. In this study, the simultaneous variation of pH and the amount of GQDs/TiO2 NC dispersed in the system was made in order to identify the best condition for efficient photo-degradation of CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg. The study was performed by exposing samples to UV during 50 min over a pH range (from 2 to 12, adjusted by adding NaOH or HCl solutions) and using amounts of GQDs/TiO2 NC from 0.15 mg to 1.2 mg (adjusted by the addition of different volumes (from 25 to 200 μL) of the 6 mg mL−1 GQDs/TiO2 NC dispersion. Mercurial species were kept at 1 μg L˗1. Experimental design levels (coded values in parentheses) for GQDs/TiO2 NC (VNP as added volume in μL−1) were 20 (−√2); 43 (−1); 100 (0); 157 (+1) and 180 (+√2) whereas for pH values, the chosen pH levels were 2.0 (−√2); 3.5 (−1); 7 (0); 10.5 (+1) and 12 (+√2). Authentic replicates (n = 8) were made only at the central point (0,0) to estimate variance. The response surface equation for CH3Hg was y = 25 VNP - 301.8 pH - 0.101 VNP 2 - 1.5 pH2 + 0.1 VNP pH, which indicated a strong influence of pH, small influence for the quadratic factors despite the slight curvature in response surface, and no significant interaction between factors. For CH3CH2Hg the equation was y = 27 VNP - 132.3 pH - 0.1 VNP 2 - 9.7 pH2 - 0.4 VNP pH that also pointed out to main influence of pH, some relevance of pH2, no relevance of VNP 2 and no relevant interaction of factors. Through the surface response and the respective contour plot, the best degradation for both CH3Hg (Fig. S5A and Fig. S5B) and CH3CH2Hg (Fig. S5C and Fig. S5D) was achieved in the acid range below pH 4.0 using 100 μL of the GQDs/TiO2 NC original dispersion (equivalent to 0.6 mg of GQDs/TiO2 NC). The observed variability of replicate measurements, like coefficient of variation (CV) at the central point (0,0) was below 0.5%.

Experimental design was also applied for Hg2+ considering even solutions at pH above 5, when it is expected the analyte to form Hg(OH)2(s) and, at higher concentrations of OH−, forming soluble ion complexes in the form of Hg(OH)n (2˗n) (aq). As the sample containing Hg2+ is added into the GQDs/TiO2 NC dispersion, with previously adjusted pH, any formed solid could nucleate into the surface of nanoparticles being prone to chemical transformations that lead to the formation of Hg2+ or Hg0. Experimental results indicated more efficiency in photo-catalytic reduction in the pH range from 2.0 to 4.0. The model equation was y = 3.4 VNP - 286.5 pH - 0.05 VNP 2 - 1.9 pH2 - 0.9 VNP pH and replicate measurements (at 0.0) was 0.2%. As for the organomercurial species, results indicated a strong dependence upon pH (best results in the acid range), a small influence of linear and quadratic contributions for nanocomposite content, that point out for the use of 100 μL of nanoparticle dispersion, and no relevant interaction (Fig. S5E and Fig. S5F).

It was chosen to continue the study with aqueous systems adjusted to around pH 3 rather than a more acidic condition as it is within the robust condition in the response surfaces. Since pH was controlled by the addition of hydrochloric acid, it is known that the presence of chloride may interfere in the Hg2+ reduction step due to the formation of HgCl2 (2−n)+ ion complexes. Besides, studies using suspensions composed only by TiO2 NPs mention possible blocking of active surface sites on nanoparticles due to the Cl− adsorption present at concentration levels of 10−2 mol L−1 [71]. In order to check if interferences from chloride (from HCl) would affect the formation of Hg0, a comparison was made using aqueous systems with pH adjusted with aliquots of H2SO4 solutions. As a result, no difference in Hg0 signal intensities was observed in photocatalytic degradation of organomercurial species and also in the photo-reduction of Hg2+ using either acids (Fig. S6).

The influence of the UV irradiation time was studied using standard solutions of the mercury species (Hg2+, CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg) added into aqueous systems at pH 3.0 and containing 100 μL (equivalent to 6 mg of GQDs/TiO2 NC) of the original dispersion. The final concentration of each mercury species was 1 μg L−1 and each tube was UV irradiated in increasing times (5 min intervals) under stirring and then, the air pump was turned on to transport the Hg0 generated during the photocatalytic reaction, to the CV˗AAS system. The maximum Hg0 signal was found to be reached at 10 min for Hg2+, at 20 min for CH3CH2Hg at 30 min for CH3Hg, which indicated a temporal separation in production of Hg0 from these different organic species in solution. Under these conditions, taking as reference the measured Hg0 obtained by chemical reduction using SnCl2, the Hg0 produced by the photocatalytic reaction of Hg2+ was 78%. For CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg, the efficiencies in the catalytic production of Hg0 were, respectively, 63% and 72% (percent values corrected taking into consideration the mercury content in the organomercurial species).

It is known that the addition of hydrogen peroxide and formic acid, in the presence of dispersed semiconductors nanoparticles, generates free radicals in solution under UV. When aqueous TiO2 or ZrO2 suspension are irradiated with photons (of energy greater than semiconductors band gap) a charge carrier pair (e−/h+) is formed also improving the production of strong oxidants (such as •OH or O2 •-) from water, especially when low weight organic molecules or hydrogen peroxide is present. It is possible that organic mercury is chemically degraded due to radicals attack and direct hole oxidation [42], while inorganic mercury is produced by reduction via conduction band electrons. However, UV-generated conduction band electrons (e−) and valence band holes (h+) tend to undergo recombination, decreasing the available number of e− to promote photocatalytic reactions of mercury species. Low molecular organic compounds act as h+ scavengers [42] affecting the spontaneous excitonic recombination, thus increasing lifetime of conduction band electrons and their chance to participate in photocatalytic reactions.

The presence of hydrogen peroxide and formic acid in the aqueous system was evaluated by adding quantities of either substance as amounts between 1 and 10% in volume. The presence of H2O2 did not improve in the efficiency in the formation of Hg0 from neither Hg2+ nor from the organomercury species. Contrary to studies that pointed out the importance of H2O2 in promoting photochemical oxidation of CH3Hg into Hg2+, because of the production of free radicals [42], it is probable that GQDs/TiO2 NC is already producing enough free radicals, directly from the acidic aqueous system, to promote oxidation of the analytes without the need for H2O2. Formic acid promoted a significant increase in the conversion of mercurial species in solution in the measurable Hg0. It was found that the maximum improvement in photocatalytic efficiency is achieved with 2% v/v of HCOOH, improving to about 30% the Hg0 detected by the CV˗AAS system. The Hg0 measured using the photocatalytic reaction reached similar levels to the one achieved by using chemical reduction of Hg2+ (Fig. 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temporal profiles of the UV induced photo-catalytic processes in the presence of GQDs/TiO2 NC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the adjusted conditions for the aqueous suspension (pH 3, formic acid at 2% v/v and 0.6 mg of GQDs/TiO2 NC), the influence of the UV exposition time was studied using standard solutions of the mercurial species (Hg2+, CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg) at 1 μg L−1. The study was made by evaluating UV exposition of the aqueous suspension in time intervals of 1 min (under stirring), followed by transporting the Hg0, generated during the photocatalytic reaction, to the CV-AAS system. Results (Fig. 6 A) showed a significant delay in the releasing of Hg0 for the organic mercury species when compared to the inorganic mercury. The Hg0 was measured, above background levels, and reached maximum value in the following intervals: from 1 to 4 min for Hg2+, from 6 to 8 min for CH3CH2Hg and from 10 to 12 min for CH3Hg, also indicating a significant improvement in reaction kinetics promoted by the addition of formic acid as the Hg0 evolution started at shorter times than the ones estimated in the experiment made with measurements every 5 min without formic acid.

The differentiated delay in the evolution of Hg0 is a strong indication that chemical speciation analysis of these three organomercurial species in solution can be performed as Hg2+ is firstly reduced reaching maximum before CH3CH2Hg photo-degradation leads to the release of Hg0 which, in turn, reaches the maximum before the releasing of Hg0 from CH3Hg.

The temporal profiles for Hg0 were also obtained, under the adjusted conditions, for the mercurial species at 1 μg L−1 (Fig. 6B–D). This experiment was performed under continuous UV irradiation of the aqueous suspensions inside the quartz tube and open to the CV-AAS system (with pump tuned on) in order to measure the Hg0 as it was formed during photocatalytic reaction, reinforcing the expected capability for chemical speciation analysis.

An additional study was made using the GC-CV-AFS system in order to understand the evolution of the photocatalytic reaction. Aliquots (25 μL) of the irradiated suspensions (at the adjusted conditions) containing analytes (at 400 ng L− 1) were diluted (40 mL) in an aqueous solution containing acetate buffer (pH 4.0) and then propylated. The typical chromatogram is shown in Fig. S7A. It is possible to observe the spectral profile (250 pg L− 1) of each mercury species at retention times (1.68 ± 0.01) min for CH3Hg, (4.39 ± 0.02) min for CH3CH2Hg and (7.26 ± 0.02) min for Hg2+ before being subjected to the photocatalytic process. After 4 min of UV irradiation, CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg remain in solution, but the characteristic peak of Hg2+ disappeared as it was completely reduced to Hg0 (that appears at retention time peak at (0.38 ± 0.02) min (Fig. S7B). The characteristic CH3CH2Hg peak vanished after 10 min of UV irradiation with about 30% reduction in the characteristic peak of the CH3Hg. In this same chromatogram, it was also observed the appearance of the Hg2+, which indicated the oxidation of organic mercury before final formation of Hg0 (Fig. S7C). After 13 min of UV exposition, only the Hg0 peak is found in Fig. S7D which indicates that photocatalysis is completed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs/TiO2 NC
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic efficiency of the GQDs/TiO2 NC was evaluated in function of the Hg0 produced from the mercurial species present at different concentrations. Aqueous dispersions containing a fixed amount of GQDs/TiO2 NC (100 μL of the synthesis dispersion or 0.12 mg L−1 of carbon content) was used and concentration of mercurial species was varied from 0.01 to 5 μg L−1 (Fig. S8). It was found that the magnitude of the measured Hg0 was directly proportional to the concentration of the mercurial species up to 3 μg L−1 then stabilizing as the GQDs/TiO2 NC probably by saturation of the nanomaterial active sites as they become occupied by organic molecules, causing a decrease in photo-degradation efficiency [72].

In order to improve efficiency to promote photoreaction of mercurial species at concentrations higher than 3 μg L−1, it could be used more of the dispersed nanocomposite. However, in this case, there is a risk to increase the turbidity of the medium decreasing the exposure of the nanomaterial to the UV. As the typical concentrations determined using the multipass-CV-AAS spectrometer is in the ng L−1 range, it was found that photocatalytic efficiency of the 0.12 mg L−1 of dispersed nanomaterial is adequate for the intended quantitative applications where sample dilution is used to minimize potential interferences.

Under the chosen conditions to perform UV-induced photoreactions using GQDs/TiO2 NC (Table 2 ), the photocatalytic degradations of mercurial species (Hg2+, CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg) were evaluated using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model [73] which seems to be the proper one, as the premises of heterogeneous catalysis, under the influence of solvent (formic acid aqueous solution), are observed. The experiments were performed with three different initial concentrations (c0) of the mercury species (100, 300 and 700 ng L−1) and monitoring the temporal profiles (area) of Hg0. The result fitted appropriately in the pseudo-first-order modeled by Equation (1) for the rate of the unimolecular surface reaction (r) and the integrated Equation (2), where –(dc/dt) is the degradation rate of mercury species (ng L−1 min), c0 and ct are respectively the initial concentrations of mercury species (in ng L−1) at a specific time (t). Besides, kr is the reaction rate constant (pseudo-first-order reaction), kHg is the adsorption coefficient of mercury species on the nanocomposite surface (L ng−1) and kapp is the apparent velocity constant of the degradation of mercurial species (approximation due to the minute concentrations of the mercurial species) determined from the sensitivity of the ln (co/ct) versus time plots (Fig. S9).

The kinetic data obtained by the pseudo-first-order model including rate constants, linearity parameters of the model and maximum degradation of mercury species in the presence of GQDs/TiO2 NC are presented in Table 2.

The conditions used for the quantitative mercury speciation, using photocatalytic reaction with GQDs/TiO2 NC, are presented in Table 1. The photocatalytic reaction was applied in aqueous standards (Hg2+, CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg) covering concentrations from 10 ng L−1 to 1000 ng L−1 (in three replicates) in order to construct analytical curves. The linear responses were modeled by the equations in Table 3 , along with limits of detection (LOD) and limits of quantification (LOQ) values. LOD was calculated as the concentration equivalent to the signal ysp + 3ssp, where ysp is the smallest possible integrated peak added to three times the standard deviation of the peak itself (10 replicates). For LOQ, the used criterion was the concentration that produced a signal of ysp + 10ssp.

Instrumental precision below 2% was obtained using the coefficient of variation of the measured Hg0 produced by the analysis of eight aliquots of standard solutions of the mercury species (Hg2+, CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg) at both 250 ng L−1 and 500 ng L−1. Intermediate precision of about 2.5% was evaluated as the coefficient of variation of the Hg0 measured obtained from eight independently prepared analyte standard solutions of the mercury species at both 250 ng L−1 and 500 ng L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of interferences on the formation of mercury cold vapor by UV mediated by GQDs/TiO2 NC
ce:section.ce:para: As transition metal ions, especially Cu2+, Co 2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, are known to affect the efficiency in generating mercury cold vapor in traditional chemical reduction processes [74], the influence of those ions the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction, mediated by GQDs/TiO2 NC, was evaluated. For these studies, photocatalytic reactions of the mercurial species were performed in the presence of 10, 50 and 100 μg L−1 of a potential interferent ion, keeping constant the concentration of Hg2+, CH3Hg, or CH3CH3Hg at 1 μg L−1. The results showed that even if the concentration of a transition metal ion was 100 times higher than the one of the mercurial species, percent recoveries were higher than 90% as shown in Table S2.

The accuracy of the method in determining mercury Hg2+ in urine was assessed by the analysis of the NIST SRM 2672a (Toxic substances in urine - at elevated levels) with a certified mercury value of (105 ± 8) μg L −1. The experimental value obtained was (107 ± 4) μg L−1 using analytical curve made with aqueous Hg2+ standards. The statistical similarity of the recovered value and the certified value was confirmed by a two-tailed Student t-test (ɑ = 0.05 and n1 = n2 = 3).

The certified urine sample was also fortified with organic mercury standards at two concentration levels (see Table 4 ) and the speciation analysis was performed. The recovered values for the fortified analyte, after photocatalytic reactions using GQDs/TiO2, for CH3Hg and CH3CH2Hg were satisfactory. The CH3Hg, CH3CH2Hg concentrations were statistically similar to those obtained by GC˗CV˗AFS (Table 4).

It was developed a simple and efficient method for mercury speciation analysis based on the photocatalytic reaction of mercury species using a catalyst nanocomposite made of TiO2 NPs modified with carbon nanomaterial (GQDs/TiO2 NC). The UV induced photoreactions, performed at pH 3.0 in the presence of formic acid as source of free radicals, enabled different kinetic for the studied reactions. Thus, it was possible to accomplish temporal separation of the Hg released form due to the different mercurial species detected by CV-AAS. The method was evaluated by the analysis of a standard reference urine sample with speciation after fortification with organic mercury species. The method can potentially replace tedious procedures based on chemical derivatization of the mercurial species that use toxic and expensive reagents being a cost-effective alternative for the ultra-trace determination and speciation of mercury.

Jarol R. Miranda-Andrades: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Sonia Letichevsky: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Dunieskys R. González Larrudé: Formal analysis. Ricardo Q. Aucelio: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.
